examples.title: Basic Frame Buffer Initialization
examples.description: Pseudocode to initialize a frame buffer for a given resolution and color depth
examples.code:
PROCEDURE InitFrameBuffer(width, height, colorDepth)
 pixels ← width × height
 bytesPerPixel ← colorDepth / 8
 frameBufferSize ← pixels × bytesPerPixel
 
 ALLOCATE frameBuffer[frameBufferSize]
 
 FOR i ← 0 TO frameBufferSize-1 DO
 frameBuffer[i] ← 0 // Clear to black
 END FOR
 
 RETURN frameBuffer
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This procedure allocates and initializes a frame buffer with given dimensions and color depth, clearing all pixels to black (0). The frame buffer stores pixel data that will be displayed on screen.

examples.title: Double Buffering Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates the concept of double buffering to prevent flicker
examples.code:
PROCEDURE DoubleBufferDemo()
 frontBuffer ← InitFrameBuffer(1024, 768, 24)
 backBuffer ← InitFrameBuffer(1024, 768, 24)
 
 REPEAT
 // Draw on back buffer
 ClearBuffer(backBuffer, BLACK)
 DrawScene(backBuffer)
 
 // Swap buffers atomically
 temp ← frontBuffer
 frontBuffer ← backBuffer
 backBuffer ← temp
 
 // Display front buffer
 DisplayBuffer(frontBuffer)
 UNTIL userQuit
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Double buffering uses two buffers - one for display and one for drawing. The swap operation is atomic to prevent tearing. This eliminates flicker as the user never sees partially drawn frames.

examples.title: Pixel Color Setting in Frame Buffer
examples.description: Setting pixel color at specific coordinates
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SetPixel(buffer, x, y, color, width)
 // Calculate buffer offset
 offset ← (y × width) + x
 
 // Store RGB values (24-bit color)
 buffer[offset + 0] ← color.Red
 buffer[offset + 1] ← color.Green
 buffer[offset + 2] ← color.Blue
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE GetPixel(buffer, x, y, width)
 offset ← (y × width) + x
 color.Red ← buffer[offset + 0]
 color.Green ← buffer[offset + 1]
 color.Blue ← buffer[offset + 2]
 RETURN color
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: These procedures demonstrate how to read and write individual pixels in the frame buffer. Each pixel's color is stored as 3 consecutive bytes (RGB) at the calculated memory offset.
